content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_006_page_001.jpg

1. When one of you has a grievance against another, does he dare go to law before the unrighteous instead of the saints?

2. Or do you not know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is to be judged by you, are you incompetent to try trivial cases?

3. Do you not know that we are to judge angels? How much more, then, matters pertaining to this life!

4. So if you have such cases, why do you lay them before those who have no standing in the church?

5. I say this to your shame. Can it be that there is no one among you wise enough to settle a dispute between the brothers?

6. But brother goes to law against brother, and that before unbelievers?

7. To have lawsuits at all with one another is already a defeat for you. Why not rather suffer wrong? Why not rather be defrauded?

8. But you yourselves wrong and defraud — even your own brothers!

9. Or do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived: neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor men who practice homosexuality,

10. nor thieves, nor the greedy, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor swindlers will inherit the kingdom of God.

11. And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.

12. "All things are lawful for me," but not all things are helpful. "All things are lawful for me," but I will not be dominated by anything.

13. "Food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food" — and God will destroy both one and the other. The body is not meant for sexual immorality, but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.

14. And God raised the Lord and will also raise us up by his power.

15. Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute? Never!

16. Or do you not know that he who is joined to a prostitute becomes one body with her? For, as it is written, "The two will become one flesh."

17. But he who is joined to the Lord becomes one spirit with him.

18. Flee from sexual immorality. Every other sin a person commits is outside the body, but the sexually immoral person sins against his own body.

19. Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own,

20. for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 6 addresses two issues — lawsuits (vv.1-11) and sexual immorality (vv.12-20) — united by Paul's theology of the body and the community's identity as God's holy people.

**Verse 7 — "Why not rather suffer wrong?":** PNEUMA connects this to the way of the cross. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's counsel is radical — absorb injustice rather than litigate before unbelievers. This is not passivity but cruciformity: the willingness to be wronged mirrors Christ's own path. The Corinthians' eagerness to assert their rights in court contradicts the gospel of self-giving love they claim to believe.

**Verse 11 — "And such were some of you":** PNEUMA calls these "five of the most transformative words in the New Testament." SOPHIA affirms: the past tense is theological, not merely biographical. The triple passive (washed, sanctified, justified) describes divine action that creates a new identity. The vice list of vv.9-10 is real — some Corinthian believers genuinely came from these backgrounds. But baptism marks a real rupture. The church is composed of forgiven sinners, not pristine saints.

**Verses 12-13 — Corinthian slogans:** EMET identifies the quotation markers. LOGOS contextualizes the Corinthian dualism. SOPHIA affirms: the Corinthians were using two slogans — "All things are lawful" and "Food for the stomach" — to justify sexual behavior. Their logic: the body is morally irrelevant, destined for destruction, so what you do with it doesn't matter. Paul's rebuttal is resurrection theology: the body is not destined for destruction but for glorification. What you do with your body matters eternally because God will raise it.

**Verse 19 — The body as temple:** EMET notes the shift from communal temple (3:16) to individual temple. PNEUMA notes the common misapplication. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's argument is not primarily about health or self-care but about ownership. "You are not your own" — this is the decisive claim. The body belongs to God, purchased by Christ's death, indwelt by the Spirit. Sexual union with a prostitute is therefore a violation of sacred space. The body is not a possession to be used as one pleases; it is a trust to be honored for God's glory.

**The "do you not know" refrain:** Paul uses this phrase six times in this chapter (vv.2, 3, 9, 15, 16, 19). Each time it introduces something the Corinthians should already know but are failing to live by. Their problem is not ignorance but inconsistency — they have the knowledge but lack the integration of belief and behavior.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
